article.title: NASA Fuels Artemis II Moon Rocket for Historic Launch, Sending Four Astronauts Beyond Earth Orbit for the First Time Since 1972
article.summary: The SLS rocket is being loaded with propellant at Kennedy Space Center for a 6:24 p.m. EDT liftoff that will carry the first crew to lunar orbit in over half a century.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

NASA's Space Launch System rocket is being fueled at Kennedy Space Center's Launch Complex 39B on April 1, 2026, in preparation for the Artemis II mission — the first crewed flight beyond low Earth orbit since Apollo 17 returned from the Moon in December 1972. Liftoff is targeted for no earlier than 6:24 p.m. EDT, with an 80 percent chance of favorable weather, according to [NASA's launch day updates](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates/).

The 10-day mission will send four astronauts on a free-return trajectory around the Moon before splashing down in the Pacific Ocean around April 10. As [previously reported](/article/2026-03/29-artemis-ii-crew-arrives-at-kennedy-space-center-as-countdown-begins-for-first-crewed-lunar-voyage-in-53-years), the crew arrived at the launch site on March 27 to begin final preparations.

## The Crew and Their Place in History

The Orion spacecraft, named "Integrity" by its crew, will carry NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman (commander), Victor Glover (pilot), and Christina Koch (mission specialist), along with Canadian Space Agency astronaut Jeremy Hansen (mission specialist), according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

The mission will mark several historic firsts. Glover will become the first person of color to leave low Earth orbit. Koch will be the first woman to travel to lunar distance and the first woman to observe the Moon's far side directly. Hansen will be the first non-American to fly beyond low Earth orbit and the first Canadian to reach the Moon's vicinity, as reported by [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

## Countdown and Technical Readiness

The 49-hour, 40-minute formal countdown began on March 30 at 4:44 p.m. EDT. Launch Director Charlie Blackwell-Thompson gave the official go for tanking operations at 7:33 a.m. on April 1, initiating the hours-long process of loading liquid hydrogen and liquid oxygen into the rocket's core stage and interim cryogenic propulsion stage (ICPS), according to [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates/).

All technical systems passed their final checks in the days leading up to launch. RS-25 engine health inspections confirmed that all sensors, connections, and diagnostics were performing as expected. The Orion spacecraft's flight batteries were brought to full charge for avionics, life support, and communications, and regulator leak checks on the crew's pressure suits confirmed airtight seals, according to [NASA's readiness update](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/31/nasa-teams-readying-artemis-ii-moon-rocket-for-launch/).

The primary weather concerns are cumulus clouds, ground winds, and solar activity, though the U.S. Space Force Space Launch Delta 45 assessed an 80 percent chance of acceptable conditions at launch time, per [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/31/nasa-teams-readying-artemis-ii-moon-rocket-for-launch/).

## Mission Profile

The SLS, the most powerful operational rocket in the world, will generate nearly 9 million pounds of thrust to propel the 5.7-million-pound vehicle off the pad. The eight-minute ascent will accelerate the crew to roughly five miles per second, according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

After reaching orbit, the crew will spend approximately 24 hours circling Earth to test Orion's systems before the ICPS fires for six minutes and five seconds to perform the translunar injection burn, adding roughly 900 mph and sending the spacecraft toward the Moon. The ICPS will separate about three hours and 23 minutes after liftoff, as reported by [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

Orion will follow a free-return trajectory, passing approximately 4,100 miles from the lunar surface on its closest approach. At maximum distance, the crew is expected to travel roughly 252,000 miles from Earth — potentially exceeding the record of 248,655 miles set by Apollo 13 in 1970, according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

During the flyby, the crew will observe approximately 21 percent of the Moon's far side under illumination — regions that no human eyes have ever seen directly.

## Reentry and Recovery

After the four-day coast to the Moon and back, Orion will reenter Earth's atmosphere at approximately 25,000 mph, enduring temperatures of roughly 5,000 degrees Fahrenheit on its 16.5-foot-wide heat shield. The reentry sequence lasts 13 minutes and includes a roughly five-minute communications blackout caused by ionized atmospheric gases, per [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

The spacecraft will deploy 11 parachutes — the most complex parachute system ever flown on a crewed vehicle — to slow from hypersonic speed to a splashdown velocity of 15 to 17 mph in the Pacific Ocean, where a Navy amphibious transport dock will be stationed for recovery, as reported by [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

## What We Don't Know

While NASA officials have expressed complete confidence in all systems, the heat shield remains one area of heightened attention. During the uncrewed Artemis I mission in 2022, the heat shield experienced unexpected charring patterns, prompting engineers to adopt a different reentry trajectory for this crewed flight, according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

Whether the 20 percent weather risk will materialize remains uncertain through the afternoon hours. If the April 1 window closes, NASA maintains backup launch opportunities within a two-hour window on subsequent days.

The broader question is whether Artemis II will validate the program's architecture ahead of planned Moon landings in 2028, as NASA competes with China's ambition to put astronauts on the lunar surface by 2030.
